description: Christological view that Jesus was born human and adopted as Son of God at baptism or resurrection.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History
notes: Early proponents included Theodotus of Byzantium and Paul of Samosata.

description: Christian communion emerging from the Church of England during the English Reformation with episcopal polity and apostolic succession claims.
citations: Thirty-Nine Articles; Book of Common Prayer
notes: Historically derived from Western Latin Christianity.

description: Extreme Arian position asserting the Son is unlike (anomoios) the Father in essence.
citations: Athanasius; Basil of Caesarea; Socrates Scholasticus
notes: Associated with Aetius and Eunomius.

description: Christological doctrine asserting the Son was created by the Father and is not co-eternal or consubstantial with Him.
citations: Athanasius Orations Against the Arians; Council of Nicaea 325
notes: Taught by Arius of Alexandria.

description: Syriac gnostic-influenced movement derived from teachings of Bardaisan of Edessa combining Christianity with speculative cosmology.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History IV.30; Ephrem Against Heresies
notes: Strong presence in Edessa and Syriac regions.

description: Gnostic school founded by Basilides of Alexandria; taught emanationist cosmology with 365 heavens and a subordinate creator god.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.24; Hippolytus Refutation 7
notes: Centered in Alexandria; significant competitor to proto-orthodox thought in 2nd century.

description: The mainstream western and universal church tradition claiming apostolic succession and episcopal order; historically the label for the consensus church against schismatics and heretics.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History; Ignatius of Antioch
notes: Increasingly used as a self-designation from the 2nd century; formalized through councils and creeds.

description: Eastern Syriac Christian tradition centered at Seleucia-Ctesiphon; developed within the Sasanian Empire and spread to Central Asia, India, and China; associated with the Addai/Thomas apostolic tradition.
citations: Synodicon Orientale; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History
notes: Often called Nestorian after 431, though the community predates the Nestorian controversy.

description: North African rigorist schism insisting on the holiness of clergy as necessary for valid sacraments; arose from the Diocletianic persecution and the traditor controversy.
citations: Augustine Against the Donatists; Optatus Against the Donatists
notes: Centered in Numidia; persisted despite imperial suppression.

description: Jewish Christian sect observing Torah, rejecting Paul, and holding an adoptionist Christology; name may mean 'the poor' (Hebrew: ebionim).
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.26; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 3.27
notes: Distinct from Nazarenes; more explicitly opposed to Pauline tradition.

description: Ascetic movement rejecting marriage, meat, and wine as inherently sinful; associated with Tatian and interpretations of Genesis and Pauline asceticism.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.28; Clement of Alexandria Stromata 3
notes: Condemnation at various synods; encratite tendencies influenced Syrian Christianity broadly.

description: Radical ascetic movement associated with Eustathius of Sebaste; condemned at the Council of Gangra (c.340) for rejecting marriage and family obligations.
citations: Council of Gangra canons; Basil of Caesarea letters
notes: Influenced early monastic thought in Cappadocia and Pontus.

description: Broad category of 2nd-3rd century movements sharing a dualist cosmology, a creator demiurge distinct from the true God, and salvation through secret knowledge (gnosis).
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies; Nag Hammadi Library; Hippolytus Refutation of All Heresies
notes: Umbrella term covering Sethian, Valentinian, Basilidean, Ophite and other schools.

description: Arian-leaning position stating the Son is 'like' (homoios) the Father according to the Scriptures, without specifying substance; favored by Emperor Constantius II.
citations: Socrates Scholasticus Ecclesiastical History; Sozomen Ecclesiastical History
notes: Dominant in the Eastern Empire under Constantius; declined after Theodosius I's Nicene settlement.

description: Position asserting the Son is 'of like substance' (homoiousios) to the Father; a moderate middle ground between Nicene homoousios and strict Arianism.
citations: Athanasius De Synodis; Basil of Ancyra
notes: Associated with Basil of Ancyra; differed from Nicene orthodoxy by one iota.

description: Early followers of Jesus who maintained Jewish practices including Torah observance, circumcision, and Sabbath; the original community of the Jerusalem church.
citations: Acts of the Apostles; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History; Jerome De Viris Illustribus
notes: Gradually marginalized after the destruction of Jerusalem (70 CE) and Bar Kokhba revolt (135 CE).

description: Syncretistic dualist religion founded by Mani (c.216-274) in Mesopotamia; taught an elaborate cosmological myth of light versus darkness, incorporating Zoroastrian, Christian, and Buddhist elements.
citations: Augustine Confessions; Contra Faustum Manichaeum; Acta Archelai
notes: Spread across the Roman Empire and east to China; Augustine was a Manichaean for 9 years.

description: Movement founded by Marcion of Sinope (~144); rejected the Hebrew Bible and the creator God, compiled a truncated NT canon, and taught a loving higher God revealed only through Jesus.
citations: Tertullian Against Marcion; Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.27
notes: Extensive network of congregations rivaling proto-orthodox churches; major driver of orthodox canon formation.

description: Syriac Christian tradition in Lebanon associated with the monastery of Saint Maron; in communion with Rome since the medieval period.
citations: Chronicle of Edessa; Michael the Syrian
notes: Eastern tradition with distinct liturgical heritage; present in Lebanon, Syria, and diaspora.

description: Schism in Egypt arising from disputes over re-admission of the lapsed during the Diocletianic persecution; Meletius of Lycopolis opposed Bishop Peter of Alexandria's lenient policy.
citations: Athanasius Apologia Contra Arianos; Epiphanius Panarion 68
notes: Overlapped with the Arian controversy; mostly resolved by the late 4th century.

description: Non-Chalcedonian tradition affirming one united divine-human nature in Christ; the theology of the Oriental Orthodox churches including Coptic, Ethiopian, Armenian, and Syriac Orthodox.
citations: Council of Chalcedon (451) acts; Cyril of Alexandria; Severus of Antioch
notes: The term miaphysite distinguishes the Oriental Orthodox position from both Nestorian and Eutychian extremes.

description: Monarchian theology identifying Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as three modes or manifestations of one divine person; associated with Sabellius.
citations: Tertullian Against Praxeas; Hippolytus Refutation 9
notes: Also called Patripassianism (the Father suffers) or Sabellianism.

description: General 2nd-3rd century movement emphasizing the undivided unity (monarchy) of God; includes both adoptionist and modalist wings.
citations: Tertullian; Hippolytus; Eusebius
notes: Umbrella term for adoptionists and modalists who reacted against apparent ditheism in the Logos theology.

description: Charismatic prophetic movement from Phrygia associated with Montanus and prophetesses Priscilla and Maximilla; emphasized new prophecy, strict asceticism, and imminent eschatology.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.14-19; Tertullian (later a Montanist)
notes: Condemned by synods in Asia Minor; Tertullian's later embrace gave it a prominent Latin voice.

description: Jewish Christian community in Syria and Palestine observing the Torah while accepting Jesus as the Jewish Messiah; distinct from Ebionites in accepting Paul's letters.
citations: Jerome De Viris Illustribus 3; Epiphanius Panarion 29
notes: Preserved in Syria and Transjordan; used an Aramaic version of Matthew.

description: Adherents to the theology of the Council of Nicaea (325) affirming the Son is homoousios (of the same substance) with the Father; became the standard of Christian orthodoxy.
citations: Council of Nicaea (325) canons; Athanasius; Council of Constantinople (381)
notes: Athanasius championed the Nicene formula through decades of Arian domination; settled at Constantinople 381.

description: Rigorist schismatic movement founded by Novatian of Rome; refused re-admission of those who had apostatized under persecution (the lapsi).
citations: Cyprian Letters; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 6.43
notes: Parallel episcopal hierarchy; communities persisted in East and West for centuries.

description: Gnostic sect venerating the serpent (ophis) as the revealer of divine knowledge against the ignorant creator; related to Naassene and other serpent-worship traditions.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.30; Origen Against Celsus 6.28
notes: Attested in Egypt and Syria; ritual use of serpent symbolism.

description: The mainstream episcopal Great Church tradition associated with the ecumenical councils and creedal Christianity; in the East specifically the Eastern Orthodox communion.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History; Nicene Creed; Chalcedonian Definition
notes: Used both as a generic descriptor and as a specific denominational label for the Eastern Orthodox churches.

description: Followers of Paul of Samosata, bishop of Antioch (d. ~275), who taught an adoptionist Christology subordinating the Logos to a divine power impersonally dwelling in Jesus.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 7.27-30; Council of Antioch (268)
notes: Condemned at the Council of Antioch (268); re-baptism of Paulianists required by the Council of Nicaea (325).

description: The emerging mainstream church tradition in the 1st-3rd centuries that eventually defined Nicene orthodoxy; term coined by Bart Ehrman to describe the proto-creedal episcopal mainstream before the councils.
citations: Irenaeus; Tertullian; Origen; Eusebius
notes: Not a self-designation; used by scholars to describe pre-Nicene mainstream Christianity without anachronism.

description: Christians who celebrated Easter/Pascha on 14 Nisan regardless of the day of the week, following Jewish reckoning; practiced in Asia Minor against the Roman Sunday observance.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.23-25; Victor of Rome controversy
notes: Polycarp of Smyrna held this practice; dispute with Victor of Rome c.195; Council of Nicaea standardized Sunday Easter.

description: Gnostic tradition identifying Seth, son of Adam, as the revealer of divine gnosis and ancestor of a spiritual race; elaborate cosmological mythology with Barbelo and the divine triad.
citations: Nag Hammadi texts (Apocryphon of John, Gospel of Egyptians); Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.29-30
notes: One of the most prominent gnostic systems; many Nag Hammadi texts are Sethian.

description: Encratite followers of Tatian the Syrian (c.120-180); rejected marriage, meat, and wine; Tatian's Diatessaron became the standard gospel harmony in Syria.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.28; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.29
notes: Tatian's Diatessaron was used in Syriac churches for centuries despite his encratite reputation.

description: Influential gnostic school founded by Valentinus of Rome (c.100-160); elaborate system of divine emanations (pleroma), the fall of Sophia, and salvation through gnosis; dominant gnostic movement of 2nd century.
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.1-9; Ptolemy's Letter to Flora; Gospel of Truth (Nag Hammadi)
notes: Largest and most sophisticated gnostic school; eastern and western branches; Ptolemy and Heracleon prominent teachers.
